8. Case Studies

8.1. Kelly Betting

Kelly criterion maximizes the asymptotic growth rate

(90)

but can be interpretted as maximize utility for a single bet if our utility function is . If our utility function is just , even for multiple runs the maximizer is to go all in when by independence. Kelly is not optimal for different utility functions.

Setup: Assume is small. Assuming 1-to-1 odds on a winning bet.

Expected log return per round is

(91)

Hence the Sharpe ratio () for log returns is approximately

(92)

At full Kelly, . This increases to . People like to use quarter-Kelly to half-Kelly.

If we use Taylor expanded log, we get

(93)

Probability of finishing below start after rounds is approximately using CLT formula.

For , , the chance of going negative is for full Kelly and for half Kelly.

The real benefits of half Kelly is that the left tail (losing most your money) collapses.

Also, full Kelly goes negative if you overestimate your edge by . Half kelly let’s you overestimate by up to . In paritcular, we have the condition for the expected log return to remain positive. This can be seen by replacing the in the formula for Sharpe ratio with where accounts for how off your estimate of is.

knowing the mean and covariance of . Taylor expanding , we get a concave quadratic optimization problem, with maximum at . This approximation is good when . For , and . Hence we get as expected.

If we don’t take a Taylor series approximation, we have a unique maximizer to the frist order equation. In the 1D case for example, we want to maximize . Taking the derivative with respect to yields

With CRRA utility: , we have the optimal weighting is . Hence fractional Kelly is equivalent to CRRA with risk-aversion = .

8.3. Price Random Walk

Efficient Market Hypothesis: If there were predictable structure in price movements, arbitrageurs would exploit it, removing the patterns and leaving randomness. Hence the increments of price/log-price should be unforecastable and can be modelled as a martingale. Empiricallly, the log-price tends to obey a random walk (Samuelson, 1965).
